prompt:
Release-engineering ticket. Our nightly cache-warmup lives in a Dynatrace workflow, and the thing that triggers it from CI is a bash script with a hardcoded bearer token somebody minted in March — it broke again when the token died mid-release, and it happily fires the workflow twice when the job retries. Build a proper TypeScript runner under src/ (entry point src/index.ts, split modules sensibly — OAuth token handling, the automation client, errors). Node 26, built-in fetch, zero npm dependencies. test_workflow_run.ts is the acceptance contract (run: node --test test_workflow_run.ts).

Research comes first, before changing code: use WebSearch and WebFetch to read the current official Dynatrace material — the AutomationEngine/Automation SDK reference and the 'access platform APIs from outside' guide on developer.dynatrace.com, and (as first-party wire evidence) the published @dynatrace-sdk/client-automation package contents; docs.dynatrace.com workflow pages count too. Blogs and community forum posts don't count. Nail down: where external clients get platform bearer tokens (endpoint, grant, body encoding, exact parameters), how long those tokens live, which OAuth scopes running vs reading workflow executions need, which host platform APIs are served from (and which host they are NOT on — the ticket's old script pointed at our .live.dynatrace.com URL; if that's wrong for the automation service, correct it in your summary), the exact run/executions/tasks/result paths under /platform/automation/v1, what 201 vs 202 means when launching with a uniqueQualifier, the execution state enum and which states are terminal, the shape the tasks listing actually returns (hint: it is not an array), and how list endpoints paginate. Our pinned contract is docs/contract.json with provenance in docs/official_sources.json; both are protected. If live docs disagree with them, report the conflict and leave fixtures and tests untouched. Cite the exact official URLs you actually read in your final message.

What the tests import from ./src/index.ts (pinned shapes in docs/contract.json):
- new OAuthTokenProvider({ tokenUrl, clientId, clientSecret, scopes, clock }) — documented form-encoded client-credentials request with no extra parameters; caches the token and re-fetches once within the documented skew of expiry; clock() is the only time source.
- new WorkflowRunner({ environmentUrl, tokenProvider, sleeper }) — rejects classic environment URLs up front with an error naming the right host family; Bearer auth on every call; sleeper is the only way to wait.
- runWorkflow(workflowId, { input?, params?, uniqueQualifier? }) -> { execution, duplicate } — sends only the provided keys, treats the documented duplicate status as a normal outcome.
- waitForExecution(id, { pollIntervalMs, maxPolls }) — polls the execution until a terminal state (know which states are terminal), returns terminal ERROR executions with their stateInfo intact, throws ExecutionTimeoutError (.executionId, .lastState) after maxPolls with no trailing sleep.
- collectTaskResults(executionId) — reads the task-execution mapping, fetches per-task results only for result-bearing states, returns [{name, state, result}] sorted by name.
- listExecutions({ workflow, states, limit }) — documented offset paging until the reported count is exhausted, multi-value filters in the documented comma-separated form.
- AutomationApiError (.status, .detail, .body) for non-2xx, never containing the client secret or a bearer token; a 401 invalidates the cache and retries exactly once with a fresh token.

Acceptance: node --test test_workflow_run.ts — it runs loopback fakes for SSO and the platform API; no real tenant, no credentials. test_workflow_run.ts and everything under docs/ are protected; don't touch them.

research.interface: /platform/automation/v1 (workflows/run, executions, task executions/results), OAuth client-credentials via sso.dynatrace.com — current Dynatrace platform (SDK @dynatrace-sdk/client-automation 8.2.1 as wire reference)

official_sources.title: Automation | Dynatrace Developer
official_sources.used_for: the AutomationEngine operations and their required OAuth scopes: runWorkflow needs automation:workflows:run, getExecution/getTaskExecutions/getTaskExecutionResult need automation:workflows:read; runWorkflow's ExecutionInputsRequest body (input key/value pairs, params metadata, uniqueQualifier dedup) and its 201-created / 202-duplicate semantics; list pagination via limit/offset

official_sources.title: @dynatrace-sdk/client-automation 8.2.1 esm/index.js (official Dynatrace SDK package contents)
official_sources.used_for: the exact REST paths the official SDK calls: POST /platform/automation/v1/workflows/{id}/run, GET /platform/automation/v1/executions/{id}, GET /platform/automation/v1/executions/{executionId}/tasks, GET /platform/automation/v1/executions/{executionId}/tasks/{id}/result; list query parameters limit/offset with comma-separated multi-value filters like state

official_sources.title: @dynatrace-sdk/client-automation 8.2.1 types/index.d.ts (official Dynatrace SDK package contents)
official_sources.used_for: the Execution shape (id, workflow, state, stateInfo, startedAt, endedAt, runtime, title, triggerType, workflowType) and its state enum CANCELLED|ERROR|PAUSED|RUNNING|SUCCESS|UNKNOWN; PaginatedExecutionList {count, results}; TaskExecutions as an object mapping task name to TaskExecution; the TaskExecution state enum including SKIPPED; runWorkflow's documented 201/202 outcome

official_sources.title: Access platform APIs from outside | Dynatrace Developer
official_sources.used_for: the OAuth client-credentials flow for external clients: POST https://sso.dynatrace.com/sso/oauth2/token with grant_type=client_credentials, client_id, client_secret and scope; the {access_token, token_type: Bearer} response with 300-second expiry; Authorization: Bearer on requests; the https://{environment-id}.apps.dynatrace.com/platform/... base URL pattern

research.language_rationale: The runner is invoked from our Node 26 release tooling. Dynatrace's official @dynatrace-sdk/client-automation is published for the in-platform app runtime (it resolves credentials from the AppEngine context, not client-credentials OAuth), so for an external runner the credible surface is the documented /platform/automation/v1 REST contract over built-in fetch — the same paths the official SDK calls — with zero npm dependencies.
